speaker: interviewer
text: April 12, 1996. Interview with Rachael Rubin nee Schmutter. This is tape four. We were talking about after you decided to leave Korets, you went to Rovno?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in Rovno? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We were trying to get to-- to Lodz. I don't remember exactly what we did in Rovno. But, we were trying to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a DP camp there? Where were you staying?

speaker: interviewee
text: In DP camp, we were in Austria. After Lodz, we came to DP camp in Linz. And we were till '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: So '48, you were in a DP camp in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austria, yeah. Six months we were in Lodz, [INAUDIBLE]. You know? And then they were trying to transport Jewish people to Austria, to Germany. You know? So we went to Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any sort of work when you were in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to courses. I mean, I took courses-- English, because I didn't know English. And then, what else did I do there? Nothing special. We-- my sister had already one baby, Irving. So I had to help her out. And then-- then, my brother-in-law, used to make a-- had-- had to have-- make a living, I mean, you know? So a lot of times, I went with him to the neighbor German to trade things. Like, we received food from United States or from Canada, you know. [NON-ENGLISH]. I think, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So we received food. So we were exchanging the food for things. But we had to-- pillow, blanket. That's what we didn't have. So I used to, with my brother-in-law, to do those things. Travel around with him, and to watch the baby, and then-- and to try to stay in line for food what we received. It was a job. [LAUGHS] You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the courses, naturally, because I had to learn something. And I sewed, too, because we had received some clothes, for instance, and the clothes didn't fit. So I-- I used to remodel things, and-- because we had to dress ourself. And that was the job in-- in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the only-- they gave the possibility for tailors, for instance, to go. So I registered myself as a tailor, as a dress maker, whatever. And-- and my brother-in-law had some [INAUDIBLE] in Montreal, the Miller family. And the Miller family-- no-- they were interested to bring him over here, to find out that he's alive. So they-- they were trying to bring him over. So my brother-in-law, with my sister and the baby, they left before me. And I was staying a few weeks, just. And then the transport from the tailors went to-- to Canada. So I registered myself, and I went too.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was actually-- they registered me to Winnipeg. To go to Winnipeg. But soon, I stopped in Halifax. So my brother-in-law and my sister and the baby was very close-- I was very close with the baby. You know? Since he was born, I raised him, practically. Matter of fact, he calls me now his second mother. So I-- I-- they wanted me to stay in Montreal, not to go to Winnipeg. And Miller had a factory, a dress factory. So the next day when I came, he introduced me right-- right into the machines and to everything. And I was living with them. Then a auntie came from the States. My-- my brother-in-law's aunt came from the States. And she gave like, key money, you know? $500. And she rented the house for us. So on the third floor, I remember [INAUDIBLE]. So we were staying there. And I used to work in the factory. And then, my sister got pregnant, and she had to stay in bed because she had bad varicose veins from the war, still.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had to stay in bed. And the baby was two or three years old. Yearling. So I-- I had to watch the baby. So the manufacturer gave me work in the house with a machine. I used to work in the house, and to watch the baby, and to watch my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had a cousin. He was there too. You know? They had a empty room. They rented the room to have some income. And we managed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we [INAUDIBLE] for a few years. '48, and '52 I got married. How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew him from-- from Austria. But then, we didn't go out in Austria. But here, you know all the-- in Montreal, all the newcomers were like, you know, an [? esplanade ?]. Tied up, and always meeting together, and, you know. So we met each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 50-- October the 19, '52.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue working after you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In the factory, and-- I was living on Park Avenue in a small house. And that's how I used to take out work from the factory, in the factory, in a-- dress store, taking out alterations. All kinds of things, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did people treat you when you came from Europe? Did you talk to anybody about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I didn't talk to them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You-- I didn't talk to them, and they couldn't understand. How-- How can you-- can you get through those things? It's impossible. You know, they couldn't believe. So I didn't talk about this. It's the first time that I'm talking. Even to my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two. Two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joey and Peter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, two. Two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ariel and Joel. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We had a hard time while we were here, because I didn't like to go to the immigration to get-- like people were getting clothes, money and, you know, they were helping out. But I didn't like those things. If I lived through a war, and I tried to work that time, so why shouldn't I work now when I'm free? You know? So I-- I worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not now, no. Now I'm retired. If you can call it retirement [LAUGHS] in a house. You know. Now I have a big house, and I'm a landlady. Three more apartments with tenants. So it's a full-time job.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think back of your life and having survived the war, how do you think the war affected your life today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I miss a lot of things, what I could-- I could achieve. But the war disturbed me, you know. That's what I miss now. Education, you know. I could, but I couldn't afford. And now, I feel like-- not financially. Financially, it's-- for me, it's not important. The big, nice houses, with the-- with the mink coats. With things like that, not for me important, no. For me, it's important to be somebody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To know more. And-- for instance, language. The French language. It wasn't time, then, to learn. It was too late for me. Or a-- a trade. What I'd liked to be, for instance. To work for somebody. To do something. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was too late to start when I came to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think your experiences affected the way you brought up your children? [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe yes, [INAUDIBLE] because I raised them differently. I raised them not with luxury. Not-- Practical. You know, in a practical way. Today the life here, it's-- it's not practical. For instance, today, the generation-- mine are different. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Peter, a little bit, fits into this generation, but Joey-- it's very hard for him to fit in because he comes from a home when everything is-- is made. And he has satisfaction. And I had satisfaction when I made myself something. To be productive. You know? So he fits in less. And this, I sometimes, I feel as if it's my fault because I raised him this way. I was like this. I couldn't fit in. And I'm not-- even now, I don't fit in in those crowds, where-- where it plays the role. Like the outfits, the, you know, the restaurants, and to go out, to travel a lot. I can't fit in. I'm not-- I'm not the type. I wasn't raised this way. Did your experiences affect the way you believe in your religion? The way you practice your religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Partly. Partly, because I was asking always the question why? Why did it happen to us? Like the other day, I was watching Moses on TV. And I was there. And I saw the miracles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are the miracles then? One, at least, miracle should be. You know? So it impacted a little bit. But still, we are Jewish people. We have to stay. We can't change ourselves. You know? And I'm still alive. It's-- it's-- maybe this is a miracle too. That's it. Did you keep in touch with anybody that you met through the war? People that helped you along the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But they help me? No. I just keep in touch with those what I went through together with them. And who was that? Like for the [NON-ENGLISH] family. With [NON-ENGLISH] [INAUDIBLE] in the window. With [NON-ENGLISH]. But she's in Israel. We were together in the-- in the forest. Then with her sister-in-law, [NON-ENGLISH], When she was injured and they took her to Moscow, she was in the partisan. I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was in touch with her. We were very close. And we were in touch with her brother. He's in-- in Hartford, Connecticut. No, in Detroit, I'm sorry. He's in Detroit. So it's [NON-ENGLISH]. So we are in touch. That's it. If you had a message to give to your children and the next generation, what would that be?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like the next generation to learn, to know, as a result of your experiences? [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to tell them. To give them-- they should be-- they should-- I mean, act, or-- the same way like I did, you know. I wouldn't want them to get through this-- the same thing, You know-- I don't think so if, today, the generation would be able to get through this, you know. We were like, you know, from iron build. But [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They are different. They are different, you know. And I would want them to be different, and not to get through this anymore. That's what should be. They should remember, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you very much for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. I'm glad I did it too. Because I never, never-- [LAUGHS]. You know, I didn't remember, even, you know, that many things in order. [INAUDIBLE] But while you were asking me from year to year, so I-- I opened up.

speaker: interviewer
text: We appreciate it. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] To the left is my son, Joey. Next, is the bride, Ann [NON-ENGLISH]. My son, Peter, me, and my husband, Abe.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: February the 20th, 1983.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Ottawa?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ottawa. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Those are my grandchildren from Peter. To the left is Ariel, 11-year-old. Next is Joel, six-year-old. Picture was taken in Calgary. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to introduce my family. To my right is my husband, Abe. To the left is my son, Joey. And unfortunately, my other son is in Raleigh. He can't be present with his family there.

speaker: interviewee
text: JOEY (SON): And I would just like to say that as children of Holocaust survivors, we've often treated our parents as, perhaps, porcelain figurines when it pertains to the Holocaust. We've often been reluctant to ask too many questions because in telling the story would mean that they would relive the experience. And we didn't want to do that. We often just-- we would steer away from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're grateful for the opportunity to-- for my mother to sort of tell what transpired. Because in doing so, it fills a large void in-- in her kids' lives, because when it comes down to it, our strengths, all that we are today, is due entirely to who, you know, to who our parents are. To who-- where we came from.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our strengths are their strengths. Our accomplishments are their accomplishments. And our lives are far more complete now, now that we know what has transpired. So thank you very much for-- for giving us this opportunity to sort of close a circle, and to-- to sort of, fill a very large void in our lives. Thank you very much.
